返回本批次

编码剂的成分

Components of a Coding Agent

magazine.sebastianraschka.com 作者 MindGods 分数 202 评论 67 2026/04/04 21:16
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 在本文中，我想介绍编码代理和代理工具的整体设计：它们是什么、它们如何工作以及不同部分在实践中如何组合在一起。我的《Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)》和《Build a Large Reasoning Model (From Scratch)》书籍的读者经常询问有关代理的问题，因此我认为写一份我可以指出的参考文献会很有用。更一般地说，代理已成为一个重要的主题，因为实用 LLM 系统的最新进展不仅仅是关于更好的模型，而是关于我们如何使用它们。在许多现实应用程序中，周围的系统（例如工具使用、上下文管理和内存）与模型本身发挥着同样重要的作用。这也有助于解释为什么像 Claude Code 或 Codex 这样的系统感觉比普通聊天界面中使用的相同模型要强大得多。在本文中，我列出了编码代理的六个主要构建块。您可能熟悉 Claude Code 或 Codex CLI，但只是为了奠定基础，它们本质上是代理编码工具，将 LLM 包装在应用程序层中，即所谓的代理工具，以便更方便、更好地执行编码任务。编码代理是专为软件工作而设计，其中值得注意的部分不仅是模型选择，还有周围的系统，包括存储库上下文、工具设计、提示缓存稳定性、内存和长会话连续性。这种区别很重要，因为当我们谈论法学硕士的编码能力时，人们经常将模型、推理行为和代理产品整合为一件事。但在讨论编码代理细节之前，让我简要介绍一下更广泛的概念、LLM、推理模型和代理之间的差异。LLM 是核心的下一个令牌模型。推理模型仍然是法学硕士，但通常是经过训练和/或提示在中间推理、验证或搜索候选答案上花费更多推理时间计算的模型。代理是顶层的一层，可以理解为围绕模型的控制循环。通常，给定一个目标，代理层（或工具）决定下一步要检查什么、调用哪些工具、如何更新其状态以及何时停止等。粗略地说，我们可以将这种关系视为：LLM 是引擎，推理模型是增强的引擎（更强大，但使用起来更昂贵），而代理工具则帮助我们建立模型。这个类比并不完美，因为我们也可以使用传统LLM和推理LLM作为独立模型（在聊天UI或Python会话中），但我希望它能传达要点。图2：传统LLM、推理LLM（或推理模型）和包裹在代理线束中的LLM之间的关系。换句话说，代理是在环境内重复调用模型的系统。所以，简而言之，我们可以这样总结：LLM：原始模型推理模型： LLM 优化以输出中间推理轨迹并验证自身更多代理：使用模型加工具、内存和环境反馈的循环代理工具：围绕代理的软件支架，用于管理上下文、工具使用、提示、状态和控制流编码工具：代理工具的特殊情况；即，用于管理代码上下文、工具、执行和迭代反馈的软件工程的特定于任务的工具。如上所述，在代理和编码工具的上下文中，我们还有两个流行的术语代理工具和（代理）编码工具。编码工具是围绕模型的软件支架，可帮助模型有效地编写和编辑代码。代理工具的范围更广一些，并不特定于编码（例如，想想 OpenClaw）。 Codex 和 Claude Code 可以被认为是编码工具。总之，更好的 LLM 为推理模型提供了更好的基础（这涉及额外的训练），而工具可以从这个推理模型中获得更多。当然，LLM 和推理模型也能够自行解决编码任务（无需工具），但编码工作只是部分涉及下一代代币生成。其中很多是关于存储库导航、搜索、函数查找、差异应用、测试执行、错误检查以及将所有相关信息保留在上下文中。 （编码人员可能知道这是一项艰苦的脑力工作，这就是为什么我们不喜欢在编码过程中被打扰:)）。图 3. 编码工具结合了三层：模型系列、代理循环和运行时支持。模型提供“引擎”，代理循环驱动迭代问题解决，运行时支持提供管道。在循环中，“观察”从环境中收集信息，“检查”分析该信息，“选择”选择下一步，“行动”执行它。这里的要点是，良好的编码工具可以使推理和非推理模型感觉比普通聊天框中的模型强大得多，因为它有助于上下文管理等。在上一节中提到，当我们说利用时，我们通常指的是模型周围的软件层，它组装提示、公开工具、跟踪文件状态、应用编辑、运行命令、管理权限、缓存稳定前缀、存储内存等等。如今，在使用 LLM 时，与直接提示模型或使用网络聊天 UI（更接近“与上传的文件聊天”）相比，这一层塑造了大部分用户体验。如今的 LLM 具有非常相似的功能（例如，GPT-5.4、Opus 4.6 和 GLM-5 等的普通版本），该工具通常可以成为使一个 LLM 比另一个 LLM 工作得更好的区别因素。这是推测性的，但我怀疑，如果我们将最新、最有能力的开放式 LLM 之一（例如 GLM-5）放入类似的工具中，它很可能在 Codex 或中与 GPT-5.4 表现相同。克劳德代码中的克劳德作品 4.6。也就是说，一些特定于安全带的后期培训通常是有益的。例如，OpenAI 历史上维护着单独的 GPT-5.3 和 GPT-5.3-Codex 变体。在下一节中，我想更深入地了解细节，并使用我的迷你编码代理讨论编码工具的核心组件：https://github.com/rasbt/mini-coding-agent.Figure 4：编码代理/编码工具的主要工具功能，将在以下各节中讨论。顺便说一句，为了简单起见，在本文中，我可以互换使用术语“编码代理”和“编码工具”。 （严格来说，代理是模型驱动的决策循环，而线束是提供上下文、工具和执行支持的周围软件支架。）图 5：最小但完全工作的、从头开始的迷你编码代理（用纯 Python 实现）总之，下面是编码代理的六个主要组件。您可以查看我的最小但完全工作的、从头开始的迷你编码代理（用纯 Python 实现）的源代码，以获取更具体的代码示例。该代码通过代码注释对下面讨论的六个组件进行了注释：############################## #### 六个代理组件 #### ################################ # 1) Live Repo Context -> WorkspaceContext # 2) Prompt Shape And Cache Reuse -> build_prefix、memory_text、prompt # 3) Structured Tools, Validation, And Permissions -> build_tools, run_tool、validate_tool、approve、parse、path、tool_* # 4) 上下文缩减和输出管理 -> Clip、history_text # 5) 脚本、内存和恢复 -> SessionStore、record、note_tool、ask、reset # 6) 委派和有界子代理 -> tool_delegate 这可能是最明显的组件，但它也是最重要的组件之一。当用户说“修复测试”或“实施”时xyz，”模型应该知道它是否在 Git 存储库内、它位于哪个分支、哪些项目文档可能包含指令等等。这是因为这些细节经常会更改或影响正确的操作。例如，“修复测试”不是一个独立的指令。如果代理看到 AGENTS.md 或项目自述文件，它可能会了解要运行哪个测试命令等。如果它知道存储库根和布局，它可以在正确的位置查找而不是猜测。此外，git 分支、状态和提交可以帮助提供有关当前正在进行哪些更改以及关注位置的更多上下文。图 6：代理工具首先构建一个小型工作区摘要，该摘要与用户对其他项目上下文的请求相结合。要点是编码代理收集在进行任何工作之前，请预先获取信息（“稳定的事实”作为工作区摘要），这样就不会在每次提示时都从零开始，没有上下文。一旦代理有了存储库视图，下一个问题就是如何将该信息提供给模型。上图显示了这种情况的简化视图（“组合提示：前缀+请求”），但实际上，在每个用户查询上组合和重新处理工作区摘要会相对浪费。即，编码会话是重复的，并且代理规则通常保持不变。工具描述通常也保持不变。甚至工作区摘要通常（大部分）保持不变。主要更改通常是最新的用户请求、最近的记录，也许还有短期记忆。“智能”运行时不会每次都将所有内容重建为一个巨大的无差别提示，如下图所示。图 7：代理工具构建一个稳定的提示前缀，添加不断变化的会话状态，然后将组合的提示提供给模型。与第 1 节的主要区别在于，第 1 节是关于收集存储库事实的。在这里，我们现在感兴趣的是有效地打包和缓存这些事实以进行重复的模型调用。“稳定”“稳定提示前缀”意味着其中包含的信息不会发生太大变化。它通常包含一般说明、工具说明和工作区摘要。我们不想在每次交互中都浪费计算资源从头开始重建它如果没有任何重要的变化。其他组件更新得更频繁（通常是每回合）。这包括短期记忆、最近的成绩单和最新的用户请求。简而言之，“稳定提示前缀”的缓存方面只是智能运行时尝试重用该部分。工具访问和工具使用开始感觉不像聊天，而更像代理。简单的模型可以建议散文中的命令，但编码工具中的 LLM 应该做一些更窄、更有用的事情，并且实际上能够执行命令并检索结果（而不是我们手动调用命令并将结果粘贴回来）但是，该工具通常不会让模型即兴创作任意语法，而是提供一个预定义的允许和命名工具列表，其中包含清晰的输入和清晰的边界。 （但是，当然，像 Python subprocess.call 这样的东西可以成为其中的一部分，以便代理也可以执行任意广泛的 shell 命令列表。）工具使用流程如下图所示。图 8：模型发出结构化操作，工具对其进行验证，可选择请求批准，执行它，并将有界结果反馈回循环中。为了说明这一点，下面是使用我的迷你编码代理的用户通常会看到的示例。 （这不像 Claude Code 或 Codex 那样漂亮，因为它非常小，并且使用纯 Python，没有任何外部依赖项。）图 9：Mini Coding Agent 中工具调用批准请求的图示。在这里，模型必须选择工具可识别的操作，如列出文件、读取文件、搜索、运行 shell 命令、写入文件等。它还必须提供工具可以检查的形状的参数。因此，当模型要求执行某项操作时，运行时可以停止并以编程方式运行检查诸如“这是一个已知的工具吗？”，“参数有效吗？”，“这需要用户批准吗？”“请求的路径是否在工作区内部？”只有在这些检查通过后，才会真正运行任何东西。当然，运行编码代理会带来一些风险，但线束检查还提高了可靠性，因为模型不会执行完全任意的命令。此外，除了拒绝格式错误的操作和批准门控之外，还可以通过检查文件路径将文件访问保留在存储库内。从某种意义上说，线束正在提供模型自由度较低，但同时也提高了可用性。上下文膨胀并不是编码代理特有的问题，而是法学硕士普遍存在的问题。当然，LLM 现在支持越来越长的上下文（我最近写了关于注意力变体的文章，这使得它在计算上更加可行），但是长上下文仍然很昂贵，并且还会引入额外的噪音（如果有很多不相关的信息）。在多轮聊天期间，编码代理比常规 LLM 更容易受到上下文膨胀的影响，因为重复的文件读取、冗长的工具输出、日志等。如果运行时将所有这些保持在完全保真度，它将耗尽可用的上下文代币很快。因此，一个好的编码工具通常在处理上下文膨胀方面非常复杂，而不仅仅是像常规聊天 UI 那样剪切我们的摘要信息。从概念上讲，编码代理中的上下文压缩可能如下图所示进行工作。具体来说，我们将进一步放大上一节中图 8 的剪辑（步骤 6）部分。图 10：大型输出被剪辑，较旧的读取被重复数据删除，并且转录本在返回到提示之前被压缩。最小的线束使用至少两种压缩策略来管理该问题。第一个是剪辑，它可以缩短长文档片段、大型工具输出、内存注释和转录条目。换句话说，它可以防止任何一段文本仅仅因为它很冗长而接管提示预算。第二种策略是转录减少或摘要，它将完整的会话历史记录（下一节将详细介绍）变成更小的提示摘要。这里的一个关键技巧是让最近的事件更丰富，因为它们更有可能对当前步骤很重要。我们更积极地压缩较旧的事件，因为它们可能不太相关。此外，我们还对较旧的文件读取进行重复数据删除，这样模型就不会仅仅因为在会话的早期多次读取相同的文件内容而一遍又一遍地看到相同的文件内容。总的来说，我认为这是良好的编码代理设计中被低估的、无聊的部分之一。很多表面上的“模型质量”实际上是上下文质量。在实践中，这里涵盖的所有这 6 个核心概念都是高度交织的，不同的部分和图表以不同的焦点或缩放级别涵盖它们。在上一节中，我们介绍了历史记录的即时使用以及如何构建紧凑的记录。问题是：有多少过去的内容应该在下一个回合中返回到模型中？所以重点是压缩、剪裁、现在，结构化会话内存这一部分是关于历史记录的存储时间结构。这里的问题是：随着时间的推移，代理会保留什么作为永久记录？因此，重点是运行时将更完整的转录本保留为持久状态，同时保留更轻的内存层，该内存层更小，并且会被修改和压缩，而不仅仅是附加到。总而言之，编码代理将状态分为（至少）两层：工作内存：代理显式保留的小而精炼的状态完整的转录本：这涵盖了所有用户请求、工具输出和 LLM 响应图 11：新事件被附加到完整的转录本并汇总在工作内存中。磁盘上的会话文件通常存储为 JSON 文件。上图说明了两个主要会话文件：完整记录和工作内存，它们通常存储为磁盘上的 JSON 文件。如前所述，完整的记录存储了整个历史记录，如果我们关闭代理，它是可以恢复的。工作记忆更多的是一个包含当前最重要信息的蒸馏版本，这与紧凑转录本有些关系。但是紧凑转录本和工作记忆的作用略有不同。紧凑的转录本用于快速重建。它的工作是为模型提供最近历史的压缩视图，以便它可以继续对话，而无需每次都看到完整的记录。工作记忆更多的是为了任务的连续性。它的工作是对轮次中的重要事项进行明确维护的小摘要，例如当前任务、重要文件和最近的笔记。按照上图中的步骤 4，最新的用户请求以及 LLM 响应和工具输出将在下一轮的完整记录和工作记忆中记录为“新事件”，这在上图中并未显示以减少混乱。一旦代理拥有工具和状态，下一个有用的功能就是委派。原因是它允许我们通过子代理将某些工作并行化为子任务，并加快主要任务的速度。例如，主代理可能正在执行一项任务，但仍然需要辅助答案，例如哪个文件定义了符号、配置说明了什么，或者测试失败的原因。将其拆分为有界子任务而不是强制一个循环同时承载每个工作线程是有用的。（在我的迷你编码代理中，实现更简单，子任务仍然同步运行，但基本思想是相同的。）子代理只有在继承足够的上下文来完成实际工作时才有用。但如果我们不限制它，我们现在就会有多个代理重复工作、接触相同的文件或生成更多的子代理，等等。因此，棘手的设计问题不仅在于如何生成子代理，还在于如何绑定子代理:)。图 12：子代理继承了足够的有用上下文，但它在比主代理更严格的边界内运行。这里的技巧是，子代理继承了足够有用的上下文，但也受到了约束（例如，只读且递归深度受限）Claude Code 长期以来一直支持子代理，Codex 最近添加了它们。 Codex 通常不会强制子代理进入只读模式。相反，他们通常继承主要代理的大部分沙箱和审批设置。因此，边界更多的是关于任务范围、上下文和深度。上面的部分试图涵盖编码代理的主要组件。如前所述，它们在实施过程中或多或少紧密地交织在一起。然而，我希望一一介绍它们有助于了解编码工具如何工作的整体心理模型，以及为什么它们可以使 LLM 比简单的多轮聊天更有用。图 13：前面几节中讨论的编码工具的六个主要功能。如果您有兴趣看到这些在干净、简约的 Python 代码中实现，您可能会喜欢我的迷你编码代理。OpenClaw 可能是一个有趣的比较，但它不是完全相同的一种系统。OpenClaw 更像是一个本地通用代理平台，也可以编码，而不是成为专门的（终端）编码助手。与编码工具仍然有一些重叠：它使用工作区中的提示和指令文件，例如 AGENTS.md、SOUL.md 和 TOOLS.mdit 保留 JSONL 会话文件，并包括转录压缩和会话管理，它可以生成帮助器会话和子代理等。但是，如上所述，重点是不同的。编码代理针对在存储库中工作并要求编码助理高效检查文件、编辑代码和运行本地工具的人员进行了优化。 OpenClaw 针对跨聊天、渠道和工作区运行许多长期存在的本地代理进行了更优化，其中编码是其他几个工作负载中的一项重要工作负载。我很高兴与大家分享，我已经完成了构建推理模型（从头开始）的编写，并且所有章节都处于早期阶段尚未访问。出版商目前正在设计布局，应该会在今年夏天上市。这可能是我迄今为止最雄心勃勃的一本书。我花了大约1.5年的时间写了它，并进行了大量的实验。这也可能是我在时间、精力和润色方面最努力的一本书，我希望你会喜欢它。主要主题是评估推理模型推理时间缩放自我精炼强化学习蒸馏法学硕士中有很多关于“推理”的讨论，我认为理解它在法学硕士背景下真正意味着什么的最好方法是从头开始实现！亚马逊（预购）曼宁（早期访问的完整书，预最终布局，528 页）没有帖子

Original Summary

  1. In this article, I want to cover the overall design of coding agents and agent harnesses: what they are, how they work, and how the different pieces fit together in practice. Readers of my Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch) and Build a Large Reasoning Model (From Scratch) books often ask about agents, so I thought it would be useful to write a reference I can point to.More generally, agents have become an important topic because much of the recent progress in practical LLM systems is not just about better models, but about how we use them. In many real-world applications, the surrounding system, such as tool use, context management, and memory, plays as much of a role as the model itself. This also helps explain why systems like Claude Code or Codex can feel significantly more capable than the same models used in a plain chat interface.In this article, I lay out six of the main building blocks of a coding agent.You are probably familiar with Claude Code or the Codex CLI, but just to set the stage, they are essentially agentic coding tools that wrap an LLM in an application layer, a so-called agentic harness, to be more convenient and better-performing for coding tasks.Coding agents are engineered for software work where the notable parts are not only the model choice but the surrounding system, including repo context, tool design, prompt-cache stability, memory, and long-session continuity.That distinction matters because when we talk about the coding capabilities of LLMs, people often collapse the model, the reasoning behavior, and the agent product into one thing. But before getting into the coding agent specifics, let me briefly provide a bit more context on the difference between the broader concepts, the LLMs, reasoning models, and agents.An LLM is the core next-token model. A reasoning model is still an LLM, but usually one that was trained and/or prompted to spend more inference-time compute on intermediate reasoning, verification, or search over candidate answers.An agent is a layer on top, which can be understood as a control loop around the model. Typically, given a goal, the agent layer (or harness) decides what to inspect next, which tools to call, how to update its state, and when to stop, etc.Roughly, we can think about the relationship as this: the LLM is the engine, a reasoning model is a beefed-up engine (more powerful, but more expensive to use), and an agent harness helps us the model. The analogy is not perfect, because we can also use conventional and reasoning LLMs as standalone models (in a chat UI or Python session), but I hope it conveys the main point.Figure 2: The relationship between conventional LLM, reasoning LLM (or reasoning model), and an LLM wrapped in an agent harness.In other words, the agent is the system that repeatedly calls the model inside an environment.So, in short, we can summarize it like this:LLM: the raw modelReasoning model: an LLM optimized to output intermediate reasoning traces and to verify itself moreAgent: a loop that uses a model plus tools, memory, and environment feedbackAgent harness: the software scaffold around an agent that manages context, tool use, prompts, state, and control flowCoding harness: a special case of an agent harness; i.e., a task-specific harness for software engineering that manages code context, tools, execution, and iterative feedbackAs listed above, in the context of agents and coding tools, we also have the two popular terms agent harness and (agentic) coding harness. A coding harness is the software scaffold around a model that helps it write and edit code effectively. And an agent harness is a bit broader and not specific to coding (e.g., think of OpenClaw). Codex and Claude Code can be considered coding harnesses.Anyways, A better LLM provides a better foundation for a reasoning model (which involves additional training), and a harness gets more out of this reasoning model.Sure, LLMs and reasoning models are also capable of solving coding tasks by themselves (without a harness), but coding work is only partly about next-token generation. A lot of it is about repo navigation, search, function lookup, diff application, test execution, error inspection, and keeping all the relevant information in context. (Coders may know that this is hard mental work, which is why we don’t like to be disrupted during coding sessions :)).Figure 3. A coding harness combines three layers: the model family, an agent loop, and runtime supports. The model provides the “engine”, the agent loop drives iterative problem solving, and the runtime supports provide the plumbing. Within the loop, “observe” collects information from the environment, “inspect” analyzes that information, “choose” selects the next step, and “act” executes it.The takeaway here is that a good coding harness can make a reasoning and a non-reasoning model feel much stronger than it does in a plain chat box, because it helps with context management and more.As mentioned in the previous section, when we say harness, we typically mean the software layer around the model that assembles prompts, exposes tools, tracks file state, applies edits, runs commands, manages permissions, caches stable prefixes, stores memory, and many more.Today, when using LLMs, this layer shapes most of the user experience compared to prompting the model directly or using web chat UI (which is closer to “chat with uploaded files”).Since, in my view, the vanilla versions of LLMs nowadays have very similar capabilities (e.g., the vanilla versions of GPT-5.4, Opus 4.6, and GLM-5 or so), the harness can often be the distinguishing factor that makes one LLM work better than another.This is speculative, but I suspect that if we dropped one of the latest, most capable open-weight LLMs, such as GLM-5, into a similar harness, it could likely perform on par with GPT-5.4 in Codex or Claude Opus 4.6 in Claude Code. That said, some harness-specific post-training is usually beneficial. For example, OpenAI historically maintained separate GPT-5.3 and GPT-5.3-Codex variants.In the next section, I want to go more into the specifics and discuss the core components of a coding harness using my Mini Coding Agent: https://github.com/rasbt/mini-coding-agent.Figure 4: Main harness features of a coding agent / coding harness that will be discussed in the following sections.By the way, in this article, I use the terms “coding agent” and “coding harness” somewhat interchangeably for simplicity. (Strictly speaking, the agent is the model-driven decision-making loop, while the harness is the surrounding software scaffold that provides context, tools, and execution support.)Figure 5: Minimal but fully working, from-scratch Mini Coding Agent (implemented in pure Python)Anyways, below are six main components of coding agents. You can check out the source code of my minimal but fully working, from-scratch Mini Coding Agent (implemented in pure Python), for more concrete code examples. The code annotates the six components discussed below via code comments:############################## #### Six Agent Components #### ############################## # 1) Live Repo Context -> WorkspaceContext # 2) Prompt Shape And Cache Reuse -> build_prefix, memory_text, prompt # 3) Structured Tools, Validation, And Permissions -> build_tools, run_tool, validate_tool, approve, parse, path, tool_* # 4) Context Reduction And Output Management -> clip, history_text # 5) Transcripts, Memory, And Resumption -> SessionStore, record, note_tool, ask, reset # 6) Delegation And Bounded Subagents -> tool_delegateThis is maybe the most obvious component, but it is also one of the most important ones.When a user says “fix the tests” or “implement xyz,” the model should know whether it is inside a Git repo, what branch it is on, which project documents might contain instructions, and so on.That’s because those details often change or affect what the correct action is. For example, “Fix the tests” is not a self-contained instruction. If the agent sees AGENTS.md or a project README, it may learn which test command to run, etc. If it knows the repo root and layout, it can look in the right places instead of guessing.Also, the git branch, status, and commits can help provide more context about what changes are currently in progress and where to focus.Figure 6: The agent harness first builds a small workspace summary that gets combined with the user request for additional project context.The takeaway is that the coding agent collects info (”stable facts” as a workspace summary) upfront before doing any work, so that it’s is not starting from zero, without context, on every prompt.Once the agent has a repo view, the next question is how to feed that information to the model. The previous figure showed a simplified view of this (“Combined prompt: prefix + request”), but in practice, it would be relatively wasteful to combine and re-process the workspace summary on every user query.I.e., coding sessions are repetitive, and the agent rules usually stay the same. The tool descriptions usually stay the same, too. And even the workspace summary usually stays (mostly) the same. The main changes are usually the latest user request, the recent transcript, and maybe the short-term memory.“Smart” runtimes don’t rebuild everything as one giant undifferentiated prompt on every turn, as illustrated in the figure below.Figure 7: The agent harness builds a stable prompt prefix, adds the changing session state, and then feeds that combined prompt to the model.The main difference from section 1 is that section 1 was about gathering repo facts. Here, we are now interested in packaging and caching those facts efficiently for repeated model calls.The “stable” “Stable prompt prefix” means that the information contained there doesn’t change too much. It usually contains the general instructions, tool descriptions, and the workspace summary. We don’t want to waste compute on rebuilding it from scratch in each interaction if nothing important has changed.The other components are updated more frequently (usually each turn). This includes short-term memory, the recent transcript, and the newest user request.In short, the caching aspect for the “Stable prompt prefix” is simply that a smart runtime tries to reuse that part.Tool access and tool use are where it starts to feel less like chat and more like an agent.A plain model can suggest commands in prose, but an LLM in a coding harness should do something narrower and more useful and be actually able to execute the command and retrieve the results (versus us calling the command manually and pasting the results back into the chat).But instead of letting the model improvise arbitrary syntax, the harness usually provides a pre-defined list of allowed and named tools with clear inputs and clear boundaries. (But of course, something like Python subprocess.call can be part of this so that the agent could also execute an arbitrary wide list of shell commands.)The tool-use flow is illustrated in the figure below.Figure 8: The model emits a structured action, the harness validates it, optionally asks for approval, executes it, and feeds the bounded result back into the loop.To illustrate this, below is an example of how this usually looks to the user using my Mini Coding Agent. (This is not as pretty as Claude Code or Codex because it is very minimal and uses plain Python without any external dependencies.)Figure 9: Illustration of a tool call approval request in the Mini Coding Agent.Here, the model has to choose an action that the harness recognizes, like list files, read a file, search, run a shell command, write a file, etc. It also has to provide arguments in a shape that the harness can check.So when the model asks to do something, the runtime can stop and run programmatic checks like“Is this a known tool?”,“Are the arguments valid?”,“Does this need user approval?”“Is the requested path even inside the workspace?”Only after those checks pass does anything actually run.While running coding agents, of course, carries some risk, the harness checks also improve reliability because the model doesn’t execute totally arbitrary commands.Also, besides rejecting malformed actions and approval gating, file access can be kept inside the repo by checking file paths.In a sense, the harness is giving the model less freedom, but it also improves the usability at the same time.Context bloat is not a unique problem of coding agents but an issue for LLMs in general. Sure, LLMs are supporting longer and longer contexts these days (and I recently wrote about the attention variants that make it computationally more feasible), but long contexts are still expensive and can also introduce additional noise (if there is a lot of irrelevant info).Coding agents are even more susceptible to context bloat than regular LLMs during multi-turn chats, because of repeated file reads, lengthy tool outputs, logs, etc.If the runtime keeps all of that at full fidelity, it will run out of available context tokens pretty quickly. So, a good coding harness is usually pretty sophisticated about handling context bloat beyond just cutting our summarizing information like regular chat UIs.Conceptually, the context compaction in coding agents might work as summarized in the figure below. Specifically, we are zooming a bit further into the clip (step 6) part of Figure 8 in the previous section.Figure 10: Large outputs are clipped, older reads are deduplicated, and the transcript is compressed before it goes back into the prompt.A minimal harness uses at least two compaction strategies to manage that problem.The first is clipping, which shortens long document snippets, large tool outputs, memory notes, and transcript entries. In other words, it prevents any one piece of text from taking over the prompt budget just because it happened to be verbose.The second strategy is transcript reduction or summarization, which turns the full session history (more on that in the next section) into a smaller promptable summary.A key trick here is to keep recent events richer because they are more likely to matter for the current step. And we compress older events more aggressively because they are likely less relevant.Additionally, we also deduplicate older file reads so the model does not keep seeing the same file content over and over again just because it was read multiple times earlier in the session.Overall, I think this is one of the underrated, boring parts of good coding-agent design. A lot of apparent “model quality” is really context quality.In practice, all these 6 core concepts covered here are highly intertwined, and the different sections and figures cover them with different focuses or zoom levels. In the previous section, we covered prompt-time use of history and how we build a compact transcript. The question there is: how much of the past should go back into the model on the next turn? So the emphasis is compression, clipping, deduplication, and recency.Now, this section, structured session memory, is about the storage-time structure of history. The question here is: what does the agent keep over time as a permanent record? So the emphasis is that the runtime keeps a fuller transcript as a durable state, alongside a lighter memory layer that is smaller and gets modified and compacted rather than just appended to.To summarize, a coding agent separates state into (at least) two layers:working memory: the small, distilled state the agent keeps explicitlya full transcript: this covers all the user requests, tool outputs, and LLM responsesFigure 11: New events get appended to a full transcript and summarized in a working memory. The session files on disk are usually stored as JSON files.The figure above illustrates the two main session files, the full transcript and the working memory, that usually get stored as JSON files on disk. As mentioned before, the full transcript stores the whole history, and it’s resumable if we close the agent. The working memory is more of a distilled version with the currently most important info, which is somewhat related to the compact transcript.But the compact transcript and working memory have slightly different jobs. The compact transcript is for prompt reconstruction. Its job is to give the model a compressed view of recent history so it can continue the conversation without seeing the full transcript every turn. The working memory is more meant for task continuity. Its job is to keep a small, explicitly maintained summary of what matters across turns, things like the current task, important files, and recent notes.Following step 4 in the figure above, the latest user request, together with the LLM response and tool output, would then be recorded as a “new event” in both the full transcript and working memory, in the next round, which is not shown to reduce clutter in the figure above.Once an agent has tools and state, one of the next useful capabilities is delegation.The reason is that it allows us to parallelize certain work into subtasks via subagents and speed up the main task. For example, the main agent may be in the middle of one task and still need a side answer, for example, which file defines a symbol, what a config says, or why a test is failing. It is useful to split that off into a bounded subtask instead of forcing one loop to carry every thread of work at once.(In my mini coding agent, the implementation is simpler, and the child still runs synchronously, but the underlying idea is the same.)A subagent is only useful if it inherits enough context to do real work. But if we don’t restrict it, we now have multiple agents duplicating work, touching the same files, or spawning more subagents, and so on.So the tricky design problem is not just how to spawn a subagent but also how to bind one :).Figure 12: The subagent inherits enough context to be useful, but it runs inside tighter boundaries than the main agent.The trick here is that the subagent inherits enough context to be useful, but also has it constrained (for example, read-only and restricted in recursion depth)Claude Code has supported subagents for a long time, and Codex added them more recently. Codex does not generally force subagents into read-only mode. Instead, they usually inherit much of the main agent’s sandbox and approval setup. So, the boundary is more about task scoping, context, and depth.The section above tried to cover the main components of coding agents. As mentioned before, they are more or less deeply intertwined in their implementation. However, I hope that covering them one by one helps with the overall mental model of how coding harnesses work, and why they can make the LLM more useful compared to simple multi-turn chats.Figure 13: Six main features of a coding harness discussed in previous sections.If you are interested in seeing these implemented in clean, minimalist Python code, you may like my Mini Coding Agent.OpenClaw may be an interesting comparison, but it is not quite the same kind of system.OpenClaw is more like a local, general agent platform that can also code, rather than being a specialized (terminal) coding assistant.There are still several overlaps with a coding harness:it uses prompt and instruction files in the workspace, such as AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, and TOOLS.mdit keeps JSONL session files and includes transcript compaction and session managementit can spawn helper sessions and subagentsetc.However, as mentioned above, the emphasis is different. Coding agents are optimized for a person working in a repository and asking a coding assistant to inspect files, edit code, and run local tools efficiently. OpenClaw is more optimized for running many long-lived local agents across chats, channels, and workspaces, with coding as one important workload among several others.I am excited to share that I finished writing Build A Reasoning Model (From Scratch) and all chapters are in early access yet. The publisher is currently working on the layouts, and it should be available this summer.This is probably my most ambitious book so far. I spent about 1.5 years writing it, and a large number of experiments went into it. It is also probably the book I worked hardest on in terms of time, effort, and polish, and I hope you’ll enjoy it.The main topics areevaluating reasoning modelsinference-time scalingself-refinementreinforcement learningdistillationThere is a lot of discussion around “reasoning” in LLMs, and I think the best way to understand what it really means in the context of LLMs is to implement one from scratch!Amazon (pre-order)Manning (complete book in early access, pre-final layout, 528 pages)No posts

评论（默认中文）

beshrkayali 2026/04/05 00:52
> 长上下文仍然很昂贵，并且还会引入额外的噪音（如果有很多不相关的信息）

因此，我认为规范驱动的生成是聊天式编码的对立面。借助 Claude Code 等工具，您可以跟踪已构建的内容、存在的接口以及为什么以某种方式生成某些内容。

我围绕相反的模型构建了 Ossature[1]。您编写描述行为的规范，它会在编写任何代码之前审核它们是否存在差距和矛盾，然后生成一个构建计划 toml，其中每个任务准确声明它需要哪些规范部分和上游文件。法学硕士从来没有看到过更多的东西，也没有积累的对话历史可供参考。每个提示和响应都会保存到磁盘中，因此可追溯性是内置的，而不是通过向后滚动聊天来重建的东西。在过去的几天里，我使用它完全根据规格构建了一个 CHIP-8 模拟器 [2]。我在 GitHub 上还有一些示例项目[3]

1： https://github.com/ossature/ossature

2： https://github.com/beshrkayali/chomp8

3： https://github.com/ossature/ossature-examples

> long contexts are still expensive and can also introduce additional noise (if there is a lot of irrelevant info)

I think spec-driven generation is the antithesis of chat-style coding for this reason. With tools like Claude Code, you are the one tracking what was already built, what interfaces exist, and why something was generated a certain way.

I built Ossature[1] around the opposite model. You write specs describing behavior, it audits them for gaps and contradictions before any code is written, then produces a build plan toml where each task declares exactly which spec sections and upstream files it needs. The LLM never sees more than that, and there is no accumulated conversation history to drift from. Every prompt and response is saved to disk, so traceability is built in rather than something you reconstruct by scrolling back through a chat. I used it over the last couple of days to build a CHIP-8 emulator entirely from specs[2]. I have some more example projects on GitHub[3]

1: https://github.com/ossature/ossature

2: https://github.com/beshrkayali/chomp8

3: https://github.com/ossature/ossature-examples

在 HN 中查看
comboy 2026/04/05 02:07
嘿嘿，看来你对此也有类似的看法。我知道想法很便宜，但听我说：

您与代理 A 交谈，它仅修改此规范，您仍然聊天并可以说“使其更漂亮”，但该代理仅修改规范，规范还可以将“显式”与“推断”分开。

当然，构建的代理 B 只能看到规格。

用户实际上可以再次关心代理 A 生成的差异，因为没有人愿意验证代理生成的充满重复的代码并通过搜索和替换创建的差异。我相信，如果有人正确执行这项权利，事情就会按照正确的方式进行。

当然，有了更好的模型规格，可以用来真正有意义地改进产品。

长话短说，行业目前所错过的以及你似乎理解的是，意图是神圣的。它应该始终被存储，最好是逐字保存，并且始终具有相关上下文（“完全正确”显然是不够的）。当前一代的法学硕士已经可以处理所有这些问题。这意味着 2-3 倍的成本，但似乎非常值得（考虑到典型的工作流程和重复，从长远来看，成本可能会低于 1 倍）

Hey, you seem to have similar view on this. I know ideas are cheap but hear me out:

You talk with agent A it only modifies this spec, you still chat and can say "make it prettier" but that agent only modifies the spec, the spec could also separate "explicit" from "inferred".

And of course agent B which builds only sees the spec.

User actually can care about diffs generated by agent A again, because nobody wants to verify diffs on agents generated code full of repetition and created by search and replace. I believe if somebody implements this right it will be the way things are done.

And of course with better models spec can be used to actually meaningfully improve the product.

Long story short what industry misses currently and what you seem to be understanding is that intent is sacred. It should be always stored, preferably verbatim and always with relevant context ("yes exactly" is obviously not enough). Current generation of LLMs can already handle all that. It would mean like 2-3x cost but seem so much worth it (and the cost on the long run could likely go below 1x given typical workflows and repetitions)

在 HN 中查看
beshrkayali 2026/04/05 03:52
是的，规格/构建分离正是这个想法，Ossature 已经在构建方面以这种方式构建了。

我同意用于意图捕获的专用层非常有意义。我也考虑过这一点，我只是不完全相信它必须是对话式的（或自由形式的对话式）。编写提示以获得正确的规范更改本身仍然是一项技能，感觉它只是将问题转移到上游，而不是真正解决问题。相对于规格的结构化编辑体验对我来说感觉更容易处理。但你提到的显式与推断的区别很有趣，值得更多思考。

Right, the spec/build separation is exactly the idea and Ossature is already built that way on the build side.

I agree a dedicated layer for intent capture makes a lot of sense. I thought about that as well, I am just not fully convinced it has to be conversational (or free-form conversational). Writing a prompt to get the right spec change is still a skill in itself, and it feels like it'd just be shifting the problem upstream rather than actually solving it. A structured editing experience over specs feels like it'd be more tractable to me. But the explicit vs inferred distinction you mention is interesting and worth thinking through more.

在 HN 中查看
visarga 2026/04/05 13:26
我自己的方法也将意图放在首位：意图证明计划合理，代码合理，测试合理。反之，测试满足代码、满足计划、满足意图。这些线程自下而上和自上而下由法官代理验证。

我还制作了单独的任务 md 文件（task.md），这使它们能够承载意图、计划，但不仅仅是复选框驱动的“- [ ]”门，它们带有结果注释，并在执行后成为工作簿。相同的 task.md 被没有额外上下文运行的判断代理、计划判断和执行判断看到两次。

我进行了测试，看看我的安全带的哪个部分贡献最大，结果是法官。显然，无论有或没有任务文件，克劳德代码都可以解决任务，但是此任务文件的存在使计划和工作更具可审计性，不仅针对错误，而且针对意图遵循。

回到用户意图，我有一个用户消息后挂钩，它将用户消息写入项目范围内的 chat_log.md 文件，这意味着所有用户消息都将被保留（用户文本 << 代理文本，这是有效的），当我们启动新任务时，将检查聊天日志以查看意图是否被正确捕获。我还用它来恢复会话中的上下文并记住我们上次做了什么。

每 10-20 个任务我都会运行一次回顾性任务，检查自上次回顾以来的所有 task.md 文件，并判断线束的性能和项目的进展情况。这可以检测任务级工作中不明显的事情，例如，当使用多个任务来实现更复杂的功能时，或者当子系统被多个任务触及时。我认为反思是可以改进线束本身以及我们如何使用它的地方。

    claude plugin marketplace add horiacristescu/claude-playbook-plugin

    source at https://github.com/horiacristescu/claude-playbook-plugin/tree/main
My own approach also has intent sitting at the top: intent justifies plan justifies code justifies tests. And the other way around, tests satisfy code, satisfy plan, satisfy intent. These threads bottom up and top down are validated by judge agents.

I also make individual tasks md files (task.md) which makes them capable of carrying intent, plan, but not just checkbox driven "- [ ]" gates, they get annotated with outcomes, and become a workbook after execution. The same task.md is seen twice by judge agents which run without extra context, the plan judge and the implementation judge.

I ran tests to see which component of my harness contributes the most and it came out that it is the judges. Apparently claude code can solve a task with or without a task file just as well, but the existence of this task file makes plans and work more auditable, and not just for bugs, but for intent follow.

Coming back to user intent, I have a post user message hook that writes user messages to a project scoped chat_log.md file, which means all user messages are preserved (user text << agent text, it is efficient), when we start a new task the chat log is checked to see if intent was properly captured. I also use it to recover context across sessions and remember what we did last.

Once every 10-20 tasks I run a retrospective task that inspects all task.md files since last retro and judges how the harness performs and project goes. This can detect things not apparent in task level work, for example when using multiple tasks to implement a more complex feature, or when a subsystem is touched by multiple tasks. I think reflection is the one place where the harness itself and how we use it can be refined.

    claude plugin marketplace add horiacristescu/claude-playbook-plugin

    source at https://github.com/horiacristescu/claude-playbook-plugin/tree/main

comboy 2026/04/05 04:01
用户手动制作的规范是理想的。

只是我们懒而已。能够聊天后，我没有看到人们回去。你不能只是将一些错误粘贴到规格中，你不能粘贴它的图像并说它使它看起来更像这样。另外，无论规范设计得多么好，诸如“实际上使其始终等待用户反馈”之类的内容都可能会在许多地方引发更改（即使是为了消除矛盾）。

The spec manually crafted the user is ideal.

It's just that we're lazy. After being able to chat, I don't see people going back. You can't just paste some error into the specs, you can't paste it image and say it make it look more like this. Plus however well designed the spec, something like "actually make it always wait for the user feedback" can trigger changes in many places (even for the sake of removing contradictions).

在 HN 中查看
ithkuil 2026/04/05 06:15
由于多种原因，规范可能是错误的：

1.你可以编写一个规范来构建一些不是你真正想要的东西

2. 你可以编写与自身或外部世界不一致的规范

3.您可以编写一个与您所拥有的工具没有足够机械一致性的规范，因此它要求您对周围的技术进行规范，而这超出了您的实际能力。

所有这些问题都可以通过在代理的帮助下迭代规范来解决。这只是一种工程实践，我们必须更好地理解它

The spec can be wrong for many reasons:

1. You can write a spec that builds something that is not what you actually wanted

2. You can write spec that is incoherent with itself or with the external world

3. You can write a spec that doesn't have sufficient mechanical sympathy with the tooling you have and so it requires you to all spec out more and more of the surrounding tech than you practically can.

All of those issues can be addressed by iterating on the spec with the help of agents. It's just an engineering practice, one that we have to become better at understanding

hansonkd 2026/04/05 06:27
我最近一直在思考这个问题。看起来大多数编码代理所缺少的是事实的中心来源。在公司正在建设和协调的真相发布之前，人们对自己所做的事情以及其他人所做的和正在做的事情有一定的了解。

现在，编码代理每次都会重新启动，由您来了解您的要求并提供反馈循环。

我认为聊天 -> 规范，然后规范 -> 代码才是未来的发展方向，而不是聊天 -> 代码。

规范 -> 代码阶段应该独立于任何人。如果规范不清楚，请让人澄清规范，然后使用规范生成代码。

今天发生的情况是有些事情不清楚，并且存在一个循环，代理开始发现一些更广泛的理解，但随后在下一次聊天中丢失了。然后人类也不会学习 为什么 他们的要求不明确。 “回忆”和特工文件都是解决这个问题的胶带。

I've been thinking a lot about this lately. It seems like what is missing with most coding agents is a central source of truth. Before the truth of what the company was building and alignment was distributed, people had context about what they did and what others did and are doing.

Now the coding agent starts fresh each time and its up to you to understand what you asked it and provide the feedback loop.

Instead of chat -> code, I think chat -> spec and then spec -> code is much more the future.

the spec -> code phase should be independent from any human. If the spec is unclear, ask the human to clarify the spec, then use the spec to generate the code.

What happens today is that something is unclear and there is a loop where the agent starts to uncover some broader understanding, but then it is lost the next chat. And then the Human also doesn't learn why their request was unclear. "Memories" and Agents files are all ducktape to this problem.

Yokohiii 2026/04/05 01:23

人为干预效果如何？您是否混合使用规范和审核编辑来进入准备生成状态？如果从任务生成代码，成功/错误率有多高，LLM 会忘记/搞乱事情还是感觉更好？

I like it a lot, I find the chat driven workflow very tiring and a lot of information gets lost in translation until LLMs just refuse to be useful.

How does the human intervention work out? Do you use a mix of spec and audit editing to get into the ready to generate state? How high is the success/error rate if you generate from tasks to code, do LLMs forget/mess up things or does it feel better?

The spec driven approach is potentially better for writing things from scratch, do you have any plans for existing code?

在 HN 中查看
beshrkayali 2026/04/05 02:00
谢谢！

> 人为干预如何进行？您是否混合使用规范和审核编辑来进入准备生成状态？

是的，流程是：您编写规范，然后使用 `ossature validate` 对其进行验证，`ossature validate` 解析它们并检查它们在结构上是否合理（不涉及法学硕士），然后运行 ​​`ossature audit` ，它将内容中的差距或矛盾标记为信息、警告或错误级别的结果。审核有自己的修复程序循环，可以自动解决错误级别的结果，但您也可以交互式运行它，自己手动修复问题，根据您认为合适的方式解决信息和警告结果，然后重新运行，直到您满意为止。由此，它会生成一个 toml 构建计划，您可以在生成任何内容之前直接阅读和编辑该计划。您可以重新排序任务、添加 LLM 注释、调整验证命令或完全跳过步骤。因此，当您运行 `ossature build` 来生成时，该结构已经是您已签署的内容。在幕后还有更多细节，我在一篇关于 Ossature 的介绍文章 [1] 中写了更多内容，可能会有用。

> 规范驱动的方法可能更适合从头开始编写内容，您对现有代码有什么计划吗？

正如你所说，现在最好是绿地。我一直在考虑一个工作流程，从现有代码生成规范，然后让 Ossature 根据这些规范工作，但老实说，我也不确定这是否是正确的模型。更困难的情况是，当工程师想要同时接触代码和规范时，我想支持这些来回保持同步，但还没有找到一个明确的答案。它在列表中，如果您有任何想法，请随时提出问题！我想首先解决我在规范编辑工作流程（和重新审核/重新规划）中看到的一些问题，特别是围绕更改如何通过相关任务级联。

关于成功率，每个任务都需要一个验证命令来运行并在生成后通过，如果失败，则单独的修复程序代理会尝试使用错误输出来修复它。重试次数是可配置的。我确实注意到，规范越简洁和清晰，有能力的模型就越有可能生成有效的代码（显然），但这正是审计应该提供帮助的。关于我上面提到的chip-8模拟器的一个有趣的例子是，即使提到特定问题的解决方案的正确名称也是不够的，我必须在规范中阐明具体算法（在这里写了更多细节[2]）。但是每个任务的完整提示和响应都会保存到磁盘中，因此当出现问题时，人们可以读取每个任务的确切提示/响应和修复尝试提示/响应。

1： https://ossature.dev/blog/introducing-ossature/

2： https://log.beshr.com/chip8-emulator-from-spec/

Thanks!

> How does the human intervention work out? Do you use a mix of spec and audit editing to get into the ready to generate state?

Yes, the flow is: you write specs then you validate them with `ossature validate` which parses them and checks they are structurally sound (no LLM involved), then you run `ossature audit` which flags gaps or contradictions in the content as INFO, WARNING, or ERROR level findings. The audit has its own fixer loop that auto-resolves ERROR level findings, but you can also run it interactively, manually fix things yourself, address the INFO and WARNING findings as you see fit, and rerun until you are happy. From that it produces a toml build plan that you can read and edit directly before anything is generated. You can reorder tasks, add notes for the LLM, adjust verification commands, or skip steps entirely. So when you run `ossature build` to generate, the structure is already something you have signed off on. There's a bit more details under the hood, I wrote more in an intro post[1] about Ossature, might be useful.

> The spec driven approach is potentially better for writing things from scratch, do you have any plans for existing code?

Right now it is best for greenfield, as you said. I have been thinking about a workflow where you generate specs from existing code and then let Ossature work from those, but I am honestly not sure that is the right model either. The harder case is when engineers want to touch both the code and the specs, and keeping those in sync through that back and forth is something I want to support but have not figured out a clean answer for yet. It's on the list, if you have any thoughts please feel free to open an issue! I want to get through some of the issues I am seeing with just spec editing workflow (and re-audit/re-planning) first, specifically around how changes cascade through dependent tasks.

Regarding success rate, each task requires a verification command to run and pass after generation and if it fails, a separate fixer agent tries to repair it using the error output. The number of retry attempts is configurable. I did notice that the more concise and clear the spec is the more likely it is for capable models to generate code that works (obviously) but that's what auditing is supposed to help with. One interesting case about the chip-8 emulator I mentioned above is that even mentioning the correct name of the solution to a specific problem was not enough, I had to spell out the concrete algorithm in the spec (wrote more details here[2]). But the full prompt and response for every task is saved to disk, so when something does go wrong one can read the exact prompt/response and fix-attempts prompt/response for each task.

1: https://ossature.dev/blog/introducing-ossature/

2: https://log.beshr.com/chip8-emulator-from-spec/

> Yeah, I did briefly consider front-matter, but ended up with inline @ tags because I thought it kept the entire document feeling like one coherent spec instead of header-data + body, front matter felt like config to me, but this is 0.0.1 so things might change :)

在 HN 中查看
AnimalMuppet 2026/04/05 04:54
瀑布有两个问题。 首先，如果实施时间太长，世界在变，而你的规范却没有变。 其次，规范中经常存在空白，直到您尝试实现它并发现规范没有指定足够的内容时，您才会发现它们。

嗯，对于第一个问题，如果人工智能可以在一天或一周内生成代码，那么世界在这段时间内并没有发生太大变化。 （未来，如果一切都以人工智能的速度移动，那么这可能不再是事实。但现在确实如此。）

第二个问题......如果 Ossature （或类似的）警告您存在差距，而不是只是弥补，您可能会以迭代开发规范而告终，后端代码生成相当于编译器传递。 但在这一点上，我不确定称其为“瀑布”是否公平。 这是规范的迭代开发，但规范就是全部 - 它是“源代码”。

There are two problems with waterfall. First, if it takes too long to implement, the world moved on and your spec didn't move. Second, there are often gaps in the spec, and you don't discover them until you try to implement it and discover that the spec doesn't specify enough.

Well, for the first problem, if an AI can generate the code in a day or a week, the world hasn't moved very much in that time. (In the future, if everything is moving at the speed of AI, that may no longer be true. For now it is.)

The second problem... if Ossature (or equivalent) warns you of gaps rather than just making stuff up, you could wind up with iterative development of the spec, with the backend code generation being the equivalent of a compiler pass. But at that point, I'm not sure it's fair to call it "waterfall". It's iterative development of the spec, but the spec is all there is - it's the "source code".

这就是为什么我目前正在考虑构建自己的简单、高度隔离的编码代理。臃肿已经很可怕了，但糟糕的决定应该让每个人都瑟瑟发抖。 十年前，人们会无休止地抱怨那些不止一种优势的事物，而这需要一瞥责任的使用。现在，每个人似乎都处于恐慌或炒作模式，忽略所有好的建议，只是为了在混乱的时间线中保持某种相关性。
That is why I am currently looking into building my own simple, heavily isolated coding agent. The bloat is already scary, but the bad decisions should make everyone shiver. Ten years ago people would rant endlessly about things with more then one edge, that requires a glimpse of responsibility to use. Now everyone seems to be either in panic or hype mode, ignoring all good advice just to stay somehow relevant in a chaotic timeline.
在 HN 中查看
HarHarVeryFunny 2026/04/05 00:36
它的核心是提示/上下文工程。该模型融入了很多知识，但如何将其取出（并使其对半自主代理可操作）？ ...您精心设计上下文来指导生成和维护状态（仍然与无状态 LLM 交互），并提供（作为上下文的一部分）技能/工具以将模型输出“缩小”为工具调用以检查和修改代码库。

我怀疑，在将半天真的用户请求转化为高级开发人员执行这些请求所采取的步骤方面，还可以做更多的工作，也许包括这样做所需的工具。

有趣的是，作者认为最好的开源模型可能已经足够好，可以通过优化的代理和一些微调来完成最好的闭源模型。我猜这个酒吧并不能真正与 SOTA 模型相匹配，但接近人类的水平 - 它是一个固定的酒吧，而不是一个移动的酒吧。通过让代理将用户请求/意图转换/增强为执行步骤来添加更多的开发人员专业知识，似乎肯定有可能降低模型需要能够从原始提示中一次性完成的门槛。

At it's heart it's prompt/context engineering. The model has a lot of knowledge baked into it, but how do you get it out (and make it actionable for a semi-autonomous agent)? ... you craft the context to guide generation and maintain state (still interacting with a stateless LLM), and provide (as part of context) skills/tools to "narrow" model output into tool calls to inspect and modify the code base.

I suspect that more could be done in terms of translating semi-naive user requests into the steps that a senior developer would take to enact them, maybe including the tools needed to do so.

It's interesting that the author believes that the best open source models may already be good enough to complete with the best closed source ones with an optimized agent and maybe a bit of fine tuning. I guess the bar isn't really being able to match the SOTA model, but being close to competent human level - it's a fixed bar, not a moving one. Adding more developer expertise by having the agent translate/augment the users request/intent into execution steps would certainly seem to have potential to lower the bar of what the model needs to be capable of one-shotting from the raw prompt.

HarHarVeryFunny 2026/04/05 01:12
如果您想要的只是一个在循环中调用模型并提供 bash 工具的程序，那么请让 Claude Code 来构建它。但你不会喜欢它！

要预览它的样子，只需告诉您的 AI 聊天应用程序您将为其运行 bash 命令，然后将“当前目录”中的应用程序更改为“在打印之前对输出进行排序”，或某些此类请求。

If all you want is a program that calls the model in a loop and offers a bash tool, then ask Claude Code to build that. You won't like it though!

For a preview of what it'd be like, just tell your AI chat app that you'll run bash commands for it, and please change the app in your "current directory" to "sort the output before printing it", or some such request.

在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/05 01:45
根据我的经验，Claude Code with Opus 4.6 经常使用 sed 进行多行编辑。最重要的是，众所周知，Pi 只公开了 4 个工具，这不仅仅是 Bash，而且比 CC 的 57 个左右的工具受到更多限制。

所以，是的，它可以工作。

Claude Code with Opus 4.6 regularly uses sed for multi-line edits, in my experience. On top of it, Pi is famously only exposing 4 tools, which is not just Bash, but far more constrained than CCs 57 or so tools.

So, yes, it can work.

在 HN 中查看
HarHarVeryFunny 2026/04/05 02:35
我认为问题/限制与上下文管理和工具一样重要。显然，bash 加上一些实用程序足以探索/编辑代码库，但如果模型没有经过专门训练以使用特定工具，并识别/适应它们的不同版本等，我无法想象它可以可靠地工作。

会话内和会话间的上下文管理似乎是一个更大的问题。如果没有代理支持这一点，您将受到模型的支配，以某种通用的方式根据需要压缩/清除上下文，并且足够聪明地决定为自己创建注释来跟踪它正在做什么，等等。

显然 CC 是 512K LOC，这看起来非常臃肿，但我确实认为工具、技能、上下文管理和子代理之类的东西都是有效管理上下文所必需的，并避免了通过告诉模型它有一个 bash 工具并进行计算可能会出现的问题。

I think the problem/limitation would be as much due to context management as tools. Obviously bash plus a few utilities is sufficient to explore/edit the code base, but I can't imagine this working reliably without the models being specifically trained to use specific tools, and recognize/adapt to different versions of them etc.

Context management, both within and across sessions, seems the bigger issue. Without the agent supporting this, you are at the mercy of the model compacting/purging the context as needed, in some generic fashion, as well as being smart enough to decide to create notes for itself tracking what it is doing, etc.

Apparently CC is 512K LOC, which seems massively bloated, but I do think that things like tools, skills, context management and subagents are all needed to effectively manage context and avoid the issues that might be anticipated by just telling the model it's got a bash tool, and go figure.

You don’t really need most of that stuff. Have sensible steering files. Have the agent keep state itself. Dont bother compacting. Its fine.

HarHarVeryFunny 2026/04/05 02:40
我认为 CC 只支持它的查找/替换编辑工具（由 CC 本身实现，使用 Node.js 进行文件访问），并且与平台无关。您是说在 Linux 上 CC 也提供“sed”作为工具吗？我无法想象它会提供“bash”，因为那太危险了。
I thought CC only supports it's find/replace edit tool (implemented by CC itself, using Node.js for file access), and is platform agnostic. Are you saying that on linux CC offers "sed" as a tool too? I can't imagine it offers "bash" since that's way too dangerous.
在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/05 04:04
是的，Claude Code 有一个 Bash 工具，并且 Claude 在某些情况下使用 CLI sed 实用程序（通过 Bash 工具）进行文件更改（尽管它具有内置文件更新），至少在我的 Linux 机器上是这样。
Yes, Claude Code has a Bash tool, and Claude in some cases uses the CLI sed utility (via the Bash tool) for file changes (although it has built-in file update), at least on my Linux machine.
在 HN 中查看
HarHarVeryFunny 2026/04/05 04:12
有趣 - 谢谢。

我刚刚问过 Claude，显然 CC 使其 bash 工具在其运行的所有平台上可用（Linux、macOS、Windows WSL、Git for Windows），并且不对 bash 命令进行特定于平台的过滤，这似乎会导致一些有趣的不兼容性 - Linux 和 Windows 上的 GNU utils（sed、grep、find），但 macOS 上的 BSD 变体。

Interesting - thanks.

I just asked Claude, and apparently CC makes it's bash tool available on all platforms it runs on (Linux, macOS, Windows WSL, Git for Windows), and doesn't do platform-specifc filtering of bash commands, which would seem to make for some interesting incompatibilities - GNU utils (sed, grep, find) on Linux and Windows, but BSD variants on macOS.

Yokohiii 2026/04/05 00:54
这个例子非常简洁明了。我不使用编码代理，但这是一些很好的概述，应该帮助每个人理解编码代理可能会产生复杂的结果，但原始交互根本不神奇。

这也是一个很好的例子，您可以将任何需要 1k LOC 的有用代码组件变成混乱的 500k LOC。

The example is really lean and straightforward. I don't use coding agents, but this is some good overview and should help everyone to understand that coding agents may have sophisticated outcomes, but the raw interaction isn't magical at all.

It's also a good example that you can turn any useful code component that requires 1k LOC into a mess of 500k LOC.

在 HN 中查看
gburgett 2026/04/05 08:41
很喜欢这篇文章。 我为我的客户构建了一个针对特定利基用例的代理（不是编码代理），但原理是相似的。到目前为止我只实现了1-4。 接下来要研究长期记忆，但我担心允许法学硕士写自己的笔记时的提示注入问题。

由于我的代理通过电子邮件工作，因此核心代理循环仅处理一条消息，然后点击 send_reply 工具来制作响应。 然后下一封传入的电子邮件再次从头开始循环，仅注入用户和代理之间发送的实际回复。 这自然会修剪上下文，防止长上下文窗口问题。

我还面临一个挑战，决定需要将哪些上下文注入到初始提示中，以及将哪些内容放入工具中。 这是上下文膨胀和工具查找成本之间的权衡，而工具查找成本可能会为每个代币支付昂贵的费用。 这里还需要考虑缓存。

如果有人感兴趣的话，完整的文章在这里： https://www.healthsharetech.com/blog/building-alice-an-empow...

Loved this writeup. I have built an agent for a specific niche use case for my clients (not a coding agent) but the principles are similar. ive only implemented 1-4 so far. Going to work on long term memory next, but I worry about prompt injection issues when allowing the LLM to write its own notes.

Since my agent works over email, the core agent loop only processes one message then hits the send_reply tool to craft a response. Then the next incoming email starts the loop again from scratch, only injecting the actual replies sent between user and agent. This naturally prunes the context preventing the long context window problem.

I also had a challenge deciding what context needs injecting into the initial prompt vs what to put into tools. Its a tradeoff between context bloat and cost of tool lookups which can get expensive paying per token. Theres also caching to consider here.

Full writeup is here if anyone is interested: https://www.healthsharetech.com/blog/building-alice-an-empow...

在 HN 中查看
IceWreck 2026/04/05 05:22
> 这是推测性的，但我怀疑，如果我们将最新、最有能力的开放式 LLM 之一（例如 GLM-5）放入类似的工具中，它的性能可能与 Codex 中的 GPT-5.4 或 Claude Code 中的 Claude Opus 4.6 相当。

人们这样做已经一年多了？ GLM 官方建议插入 Claude Code https://docs.z.ai/devpack/tool/claude 任何模型都可以插入 Codex CLI （它是开源的，可以通过配置文件设置）。

> This is speculative, but I suspect that if we dropped one of the latest, most capable open-weight LLMs, such as GLM-5, into a similar harness, it could likely perform on par with GPT-5.4 in Codex or Claude Opus 4.6 in Claude Code.

People have been doing that for over a year already? GLM officially recommends plugging into Claude Code https://docs.z.ai/devpack/tool/claude and any model can be plugged into Codex CLI (it's open source and can be set via config file).

sweetjuly 2026/04/05 04:47
担心什么？在“管理其他程序的填充程序”的上下文中，线束往往相当常见（请参阅：“测试线束”、“模糊化线束”等）

在 HN 中查看
hsaliak 2026/04/05 06:36
工具输出截断有很大帮助，是减少上下文膨胀的最佳方法之一。在我的编码代理中，上下文是从 SQLite 组装的。如果需要的话，我会在消息 ID 后加上后缀来补充被截断的工具调用，而且效果很好。 我对上下文管理的探索主要记录在这里 https://github.com/hsaliak/std_slop/blob/main/docs/CONTEXT_M...
Tool output truncation helps a lot and is one of the best ways to reduce context bloat. In my coding agent the context is assembled from SQLite. I suffix the message ID to rehydrate the truncated tool call if it’s needed and it works great. My exploration on context management is mostly documented here https://github.com/hsaliak/std_slop/blob/main/docs/CONTEXT_M...
在 HN 中查看

Compounding is probably the break point, one agent's output is another agent's input, does the garbage in garbage out rule apply?

MrScruff 2026/04/05 00:29
> 这是推测性的，但我怀疑，如果我们将最新、最有能力的开放式 LLM 之一（例如 GLM-5）放入类似的工具中，它的性能可能与 Codex 中的 GPT-5.4 或 Claude Code 中的 Claude Opus 4.6 相当。

除非我误解了这里所描述的内容，否则使用不同的后端模型运行 Claude Code 是很常见的。

https://docs.z.ai/scenario-example/develop-tools/claude

根据我的经验，它的性能与 Anthropic 的模型不相上下。

> This is speculative, but I suspect that if we dropped one of the latest, most capable open-weight LLMs, such as GLM-5, into a similar harness, it could likely perform on par with GPT-5.4 in Codex or Claude Opus 4.6 in Claude Code.

Unless I'm misunderstanding what's being described here, running Claude Code with different backend models is pretty common.

https://docs.z.ai/scenario-example/develop-tools/claude

It doesn't perform on par with Anthropic's models in my experience.

在 HN 中查看
barnabee 2026/04/05 04:15
我发现，在某些项目中，OpenCode 中的 Sonnet 4.6 可以完成的工作中，70-80% 可以使用 MiMo V2 Pro 或类似的更便宜的模型来完成。在其他方面，十四行诗完全胜过。我不知道为什么。我只发现 Opus 在 5% 的情况下值得额外付费。

我还发现 OpenCode 是 彻底地 比 Claude Code 更好，就我购买 OpenRouter API 积分而不是 Claude Max 而言，因为 Claude Code 还不够好。

坦率地说，我对 OpenCode 可以使用一些自定义命令（仅用于常见的事情，例如进行质量审查等）以及可能是一两个额外的“代理”定义来做什么感到惊讶。对于许多项目来说，甚至大部分都是不必要的。通常我只是要求它编写一个 AGENTS.md，其中封装了良好的开发工作流程、git 分支/提交策略、测试和质量标准，以及 ROADMAP.md 以及带有阶段和任务跟踪的每个里程碑的降价文件，这就足够了。

我对这些更复杂的自动化或强制执行的安全带有点感兴趣，但我不知道它们会给我带来很多我没有的东西，而且我认为与不太具体的东西相比，它们很难跟上最先进的技术。

I've found that on some projects maybe 70-80% of what can be done with Sonnet 4.6 in OpenCode can be done with a cheaper model like MiMo V2 Pro or similar. On others Sonnet completely outperforms. I'm not sure why. I only find Opus to be worth the extra cost maybe 5% of the time.

I also find OpenCode to be drastically better than Claude Code, to the extent that I'm buying OpenRouter API credits rather than Claude Max because Claude Code just isn't good enough.

I'm frankly amazed at what OpenCode can do with a few custom commands (just for common things like doing a quality review, etc.), and maybe an extra "agent" definition or two. For many projects even most of this isn't necessary. Often I just ask it to write an AGENTS.md that encapsulates a good development workflow, git branch/commit policy, testing and quality standards, and ROADMAP.md plus per milestone markdown files with phases and task tracking, and this is enough.

I'm somewhat interested in these more involved harnesses that automated or enforce more, but I don't know that they'd give me much that I don't have and I think they'd be tough to keep up with the state of the art compared to something less specific.

在 HN 中查看
kamikazeturtles 2026/04/05 00:33
> 根据我的经验，它的性能与 Anthropic 的模型不相上下。

您认为为什么会出现这种情况？ Anthropic 的模型是否更好，或者他们是否训练模型以某种方式更好地与安全带配合使用？

> It doesn't perform on par with Anthropic's models in my experience.

Why do you think that is the case? Is Anthropic's models just better or do they train the models to somehow work better with the harness?

在 HN 中查看
mmargenot 2026/04/05 00:47
现在，通过强化学习改进“循环”代理系统中的模型更为常见。 Anthropic [很可能]在后端这样做，以系统地提高模型的性能，特别是使用他们的工具。我在 Block 上与 Goose 一起使用了更经典的训练后方法来完成此任务，因为那是在强化学习作为一种方法真正成为主流之前。

如果您想了解一些相关工具和流程，请查看验证程序（https://github.com/PrimeIntellect-ai/verifiers）、爱马仕（https://github.com/nousresearch/hermes-agent）和随附的跟踪数据集（https://huggingface.co/datasets/kai-os/carnice-glm5-hermes-t...）以及其他开源工具和工具。

It is more common now to improve models in agentic systems "in the loop" with reinforcement learning. Anthropic is [very likely] doing this in the backend to systematically improve the performance of their models specifically with their tools. I've done this with Goose at Block with more classic post-training approaches because it was before RL really hit the mainstream as an approach for this.

If you want to look at some of the tooling and process for this, check out verifiers (https://github.com/PrimeIntellect-ai/verifiers), hermes (https://github.com/nousresearch/hermes-agent) and accompanying trace datasets (https://huggingface.co/datasets/kai-os/carnice-glm5-hermes-t...), and other open source tools and harnesses.

MrScruff 2026/04/05 00:38
这是一个很好的问题，我自己也想知道。我没有将 GLM-5 与 CC 一起使用，但我使用过相当多的 GLM-4.7，经常与 Sonnet/Opus 来回交换。差异相当明显 - 有时，当我认为自己正在使用 Sonnet 时，我会错误地让 GLM 保持运行状态，并且仅根据问题解决能力的差距就可以很快判断出来。
It's a good question, I've wondered that myself. I haven't used GLM-5 with CC but I've used GLM-4.7 a fair amount, often swapping back and forth with Sonnet/Opus. The difference is fairly obvious - on occasions I've mistakenly left GLM enabled running when I thought I was using Sonnet, and could tell pretty quickly just based on the gap in problem solving ability.

vidarh 2026/04/05 01:42
如果有的话，安全带对于其他模型来说更重要，因为它们有多愚蠢……您可以用安全带来弥补一些愚蠢（但绝不是全部），这些安全带试图以例如以下方式进行补偿： Claude Code 没有这样做，因为对于 Anthropics 自己的模型没有必要这样做。
The harness if anything matters more with those other models because of how much dumber they are... You can compensate for some of the stupidity (but by no means all) with harnesses that tries to compensate in ways that e.g. Claude Code does not because it isn't necessary to do so for Anthropics own models.

crustycoder 2026/04/05 00:41
及时的链接 - 我上周刚刚未能获得 ChatGPT 技能来生成可重复的管理报告工作流程。我已经弄清楚了原因，并且这篇文章几乎证实了我关于“纯”LLMS 的优点和缺点以及如何解决它们的结论。本文针对的问题领域略有不同，但解决这些问题所需的一般问题和架构似乎非常相似。
A timely link - I've just spent the last week failing to get a ChatGPT Skill to produce a reproducible management reporting workflow. I've figured out why and this article pretty much confirms my conclusions about the strengths & weaknesses of "pure" LLMS, and how to work around them. This article is for a slightly different problem domain, but the general problems and architecture needed to address them seem very similar.
